title: Developer Notes
subtitle: What to use now and what not to assume
summary: Implementation guidance for developers working with Sutton SignWriting data, conversion, symbol identity, and Unicode-facing workflows.
synopsis: These notes are for developers. They explain what to store, what to treat as canonical, why naming characters is not the same as preserving stable symbols or written signs, where official Unicode SignWriting falls short, and how FSW and SWU should be used in current tooling, interchange, and data workflows.

slides.title: Current Implementation Baseline
slides.summary: Use FSW as canonical production text and SWU as the supported Unicode-oriented isomorphic form.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for developer notes baseline.
bullets: FSW for canonical storage and processing
SWU for Unicode-oriented interchange and inspection
ISWA 2010 as the symbol reference layer
Official Unicode SignWriting is not a drop-in replacement
slides.speech: The implementation baseline is simple. Use FSW as canonical production text. Use SWU when a Unicode-oriented isomorphic form is useful for display, interchange, or downstream workflows. Use ISWA 2010 as the symbol reference layer. This keeps canonical text, stable symbol identity, and Unicode-oriented interchange in the right relationship. Do not assume the official Unicode SignWriting model can replace the current stack without redesign.
slides.notes: This is the main recommendation slide. Keep it crisp.

slides.title: Keep the Encoding Boundaries Visible
slides.summary: A healthy codebase makes canonical, interchange, compatibility, and experimental layers explicit.
bullets: Canonical text should be explicit
Interchange forms should be named clearly
Compatibility support should not masquerade as canonical storage
Experimental paths should stay isolated
slides.speech: A recurring software mistake is to let multiple encodings coexist without clear boundaries. That makes every downstream feature more fragile. A healthier architecture names the layers directly: what is canonical, what is interchange, what is compatibility support, and what is experimental. When those boundaries stay visible, conversion, validation, search, and migration decisions become more honest and more testable.
slides.notes: This is an architecture hygiene slide.
slides.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Codebase] --> B[Canonical]
  A --> C[Interchange]
  A --> D[Compatibility]
  A --> E[Experimental]
  B --> F[Stable core decisions]
  C --> G[Explicit conversion]
  D --> H[Constrained support]
  E --> I[Isolation and testing]

  classDef node fill:#eff6ff,color:black,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px;
  class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I node;

slides.title: Why Character Naming And Fonts Are Not Enough
slides.summary: Developers need more than named characters and fonts. They need stable symbol identity, predictable storage, search, sorting, and interoperability.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for not only a rendering problem.
bullets: Rendering matters but is not the main issue
Naming characters is not the same as preserving stable symbols
The deeper issue is interoperability
Facial composition shows that fonts can mediate symbol identity
Storage and search need stable models
slides.speech: It is tempting to frame the Unicode problem as a rendering problem because rendering is the most visible failure point. But developers need more than visible output, and they need more than named characters. They need stable symbol identity, predictable storage, reliable interchange, searchability, and sorting. The facial model makes the risk obvious: if the font partly determines the final symbol result, the model is no longer only about drawing. It is about data identity and interoperability.
slides.notes: Use this to stop the conversation from collapsing into font talk.

slides.title: The Safe Migration Test
slides.summary: Do not migrate existing data unless the compatibility questions are clearly answered first.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for safe migration test.
bullets: Are stable writer-selected symbols preserved across the full system?
Can full signs be represented without loss?
Can sorting be preserved?
Can fonts and renderers still work?
Can round-tripping be guaranteed?
slides.speech: If someone asks whether to migrate existing Sutton SignWriting data to the official Unicode model, the safe answer is not yet. Migration should be justified, not assumed. The burden of proof is on the migration path. Are stable writer-selected symbols preserved across the full system? Can full signs be represented without loss? Can sorting stay stable? Can current renderers and tools still work? Can round-tripping be guaranteed? If the answers are not clearly yes, migration is not yet justified.
slides.notes: This slide is practical and cautionary.

slides.title: Current AI and Data Reality
slides.summary: The computational question is now more about dataset quality and scale than about whether usable encodings exist.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for AI and data reality.
bullets: FSW remains strong canonical text
SWU is usable for AI-oriented workflows
The main constraint is data scale and quality
Unicode incompleteness is real, but not computational impossibility
slides.speech: A useful correction for developers is that Unicode incompleteness is not the same thing as computational impossibility. The current stack is still usable. FSW remains a strong canonical form, and SWU is workable for AI-oriented preparation and model input. The limiting factor now is often dataset size and dataset quality rather than the absence of any usable encoding at all.
slides.notes: This keeps the developer lane contemporary without losing the standards critique.

slides.title: What `@sutton-signwriting/unicode8` Is For
slides.summary: Bridge packages are useful, but they should be described as compatibility tooling rather than proof that the deeper problem is solved.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for unicode8 package role.
bullets: The package processes official Unicode 8 SignWriting where needed
It also bridges FSW and SWU
That makes it useful in real software
Its existence does not settle the architectural question
slides.speech: The `@sutton-signwriting/unicode8` package is useful and should be described honestly. It helps process official Unicode 8 SignWriting characters where they exist, and it bridges them with FSW and SWU in JavaScript tooling. That makes it valuable. But it should be framed as compatibility tooling and bridge tooling, not as evidence that naming characters, preserving stable symbols, and encoding complete written signs have all been solved by one model.
slides.notes: End by acknowledging practical tooling without surrendering the larger position.
